inquiries.title: HCP Merged or Moved to Another Location - Rep Sees Volume Drop and Doesn't Understand Why
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I'm in Territory DAL-11 and Dr. Reyes has been one of my top HIV prescribers for the past 18 months. He's been writing 10 to 12 units of Biktarvy a month consistently. About 3 weeks ago his volume completely disappeared from my dashboard. I called his office and he's still there, still seeing patients, still writing. Someone on my team mentioned something about his record getting merged but I don't know what that means or whether I'm still getting credit."
inquiries.what_happened: In IQVIA OneKey, an HCP 'merge' occurs when two separate HCP records - typically representing the same physician at two different practice locations or under two different name formats - are identified as duplicates and consolidated into a single master record. When a merge occurs, one NPI or location record is retired and its prescribing activity is re-attributed to the surviving master record. If the surviving record maps to a different territory, the volume that was previously crediting to this rep disappears from their dashboard. An HCP 'move' is different: the physician has relocated their primary practice to a new address, which may fall in a different territory ZIP code. Depending on whether the CRM has synced the OneKey address update and how the alignment file maps the new ZIP code, the rep may lose territory ownership of the HCP entirely - along with all future prescribing credit. In both scenarios, the rep experiences the same symptom: their numbers drop with no clear explanation in the CRM. Specialty crediting context: if the affected HCP is a creditable specialty (ID, HIV Medicine, Internal Medicine, Family Medicine), the volume loss has direct IC impact. The steward must verify not only what happened to the HCP record but also whether the credit flag traveled correctly to the surviving or new record.
steward_steps: Check the DCR log first: search for any merge, address update, or record retirement DCRs submitted for the rep's territory or for the HCP NPI in question within the past 30 days.

Pull the IQVIA OneKey record history for the affected HCP NPI: determine whether a merge occurred (two records consolidated), a primary address change occurred (practice relocated), or both. Confirm the effective date of the change.

If a merge occurred: identify the retired NPI/record and the surviving master NPI. Confirm which territory the surviving master NPI maps to in the current alignment file. If the surviving record maps to a different territory, this is the source of the volume drop.

If a move occurred: confirm the new primary practice address and ZIP code in IQVIA OneKey. Cross-reference the ZIP code against the alignment file to determine which territory it maps to and whether it falls in the rep's territory or an adjacent one.

Pull DDD prescribing data for the HCP NPI (both retired and master, if a merge): confirm that prescribing volume has continued post-merge or post-move and identify which territory is now receiving the credit.

Confirm the HCP's specialty code in IQVIA OneKey and verify the Credit flag in the sales crediting flag file is active on the surviving or current record. A merge or address update can sometimes cause the Credit flag to reset - verify it was carried over correctly.

If volume has moved to another territory incorrectly, or if the merge consolidated volume away from this rep despite the HCP's primary prescribing activity occurring in this rep's geography, prepare an alignment exception request and submit a DCR.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We investigated Dr. Reyes's record and confirmed what happened. Dr. Carlos Reyes (NPI 1847203944) had two active records in IQVIA OneKey - his original record tied to his solo practice at 4820 Greenville Ave, Dallas (ZIP 75206, Territory DAL-11) and a second record created 8 weeks ago when he began seeing patients at UT Southwestern's infectious disease outpatient clinic (ZIP 75390, Territory DAL-19). In the most recent OneKey monthly refresh on 02/15, OneKey identified the two records as duplicates and merged them, retiring the Greenville Ave record and designating the UT Southwestern record as the master. Because the surviving master record's primary address is ZIP 75390 - which maps to Territory DAL-19 in the current alignment file - the CRM automatically reassigned Dr. Reyes to DAL-19. This is what caused his volume to disappear from your dashboard on approximately 02/17. His prescribing has not changed. DDD data shows he wrote 11 units of Biktarvy in February, all of which are now crediting to Territory DAL-19 instead of DAL-11. Dr. Reyes is an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44, Credit) - every script he writes is fully creditable. The Credit flag transferred correctly to the surviving master record. We reviewed the prescribing location data: approximately 70% of Dr. Reyes's Biktarvy prescribing activity in the past 6 months is associated with patients seen at the Greenville Ave location - your territory. DCR #DCR-2024-1412 has been submitted requesting an alignment exception to restore Dr. Reyes to Territory DAL-11, citing primary prescribing location as the override basis and the 70/30 DDD volume split as supporting evidence. Decision expected within 3 business days. In the meantime, the 11 units credited to DAL-19 in February have been flagged to the IC team for correction pending the DCR outcome. Your call history against Dr. Reyes is fully intact in the CRM.

inquiries.title: Rep Asking What Their Current Attainment Is and Why It Looks the Way It Does
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I'm in Territory CHI-14 and my dashboard is showing 74% attainment for Q1 as of last Friday. I expected to be closer to 90% - I've been in front of my top accounts all quarter and I know Dr. Hernandez and Dr. Okafor have both been writing heavily. Can you walk me through exactly what's being counted and whether anything is missing?"
inquiries.what_happened: This is the most common category of field inquiry - a rep who wants a plain-language explanation of their current attainment number. Unlike a formal IC dispute, the rep is not claiming a specific error. They are asking: what is my attainment made up of, why does it look the way it does, and is there anything missing that should be there? Attainment is calculated as: IC-credited sales volume divided by the rep's assigned quota for the period, expressed as a percentage. The credited volume is determined by applying the sales crediting flag (Credit / No-Credit per HCP) to the 867 and DDD transaction data mapped to the rep's territory in the alignment file. Not all volume the rep observes on their dashboard is credited - non-creditable specialty HCPs, 340B-excluded accounts, and transactions outside the territory mapping all reduce the credited total. The most common reasons a rep's attainment looks lower than expected are: (a) data timing lag - recent scripts not yet loaded into the 867 or DDD feed; (b) non-creditable specialty HCPs whose DDD volume is visible on the dashboard but excluded from IC; (c) a recent territory or alignment change that removed HCPs mid-cycle; (d) a 340B exclusion applied to an account.
steward_steps: Pull the rep's current IC attainment summary from the IC system: capture the credited sales volume, quota, and attainment percentage for the current period.
Pull the 867 and DDD transaction data for the rep's territory: confirm the most recent data load date so the rep understands whether recent activity is reflected.

Identify any non-creditable specialty HCPs in the rep's territory whose DDD volume is visible on the dashboard but excluded from IC. Quantify their volume so the rep understands how much of their visible dashboard total is not counting toward attainment.
Check the alignment file for any mid-cycle territory changes: confirm whether any HCPs were added or removed since the start of the IC period and how that affected the credit base.
Identify any 340B-flagged accounts in the territory whose volume is excluded from IC. Confirm the exclusion is correctly applied.

Summarise the attainment build-up in plain language: total dashboard volume → minus non-creditable specialty volume → minus 340B exclusions → minus alignment-excluded HCPs → equals IC-credited volume → divided by quota → equals attainment percentage.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: Your current Q1 attainment for Territory CHI-14 is 74% as of the 867 load dated 03/07. Your quota for the period is 180 units of Biktarvy. The IC system is crediting you 133 units - here is exactly how that number is built and where the gap comes from. Your dashboard shows 161 total units dispensed in CHI-14 for Q1. Of those, 28 units are attributed to Dr. Marcus Webb (NPI 1847293015), a Rheumatologist (code 31). Rheumatology is a non-creditable specialty for Biktarvy - his scripts are visible on your dashboard because they flow through the DDD feed, but they generate zero IC credit. Removing his 28 units brings your creditable base to 133 units, which is exactly what the IC system shows at 74% attainment. Dr. Hernandez (NPI 1728304951, Infectious Disease, code 44, Credit) and Dr. Okafor (NPI 1639482710, Internal Medicine, code 11, Credit) are both correctly crediting to CHI-14. Their combined Q1 volume through 03/07 is 89 units. However, the 867 feed load covers through 03/07 - any scripts written or filled in the last 4 days have not yet appeared. Based on typical weekly volume for CHI-14, you can expect an additional 8-12 units to load in the next refresh on 03/14. There are no configuration errors or missing credits. Your attainment is calculating correctly. At current trajectory with the pending load, you are on track for approximately 79-81% attainment by end of Q1. The 16-point gap between your expectation and current system figure is fully explained by Dr. Webb's non-creditable volume and the data lag on the most recent week.

inquiries.title: Shipment Visible in 867 Alert but Dispense Not Showing in DDD - Rep Wants to Know Where the Volume Is
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I'm in Territory SFO-09 and I got a shipment alert on 02/18 that said 30 units of Sunlenca were shipped to Pacific Infusion Center on Mission Street. It's now 03/11 and I still don't see anything on my dashboard. My manager is asking me where that volume is. Which prescriber should it show under and when will it actually appear?"
inquiries.what_happened: This is one of the most common and misunderstood inquiry types in the HIV portfolio. The rep is conflating two entirely separate data events: the 867 shipment event (a sell-in transaction - product shipped from distributor to specialty pharmacy or account) and the DDD dispensed Rx event (a sell-out transaction - product dispensed from pharmacy to a specific patient under a specific prescriber's order). These are different data flows with different timing, different granularity, and different attribution logic. The 867 data captures the physical movement of product from a distributor to a ship-to location (an account, specialty pharmacy, or clinic). It does not identify which prescriber ordered the product, which patient will receive it, or whether it has been dispensed yet. A 867 shipment alert means product arrived at the account - it does not mean a dispense has occurred. The IQVIA DDD data captures dispensed prescriptions at the prescriber level, with a typical 4-6 week reporting lag from the date of dispense. This means even if the shipment was immediately dispensed after arrival, the DDD record for that Rx will not appear on the rep's dashboard for several more weeks. Additionally, not every shipment results in a dispense within the rep's reporting period. Specialty pharmacies carry buffer stock - a shipment may represent restocking inventory rather than a specific patient fill. Specialty crediting context: DDD credit is attributed at the prescriber NPI level. Once the dispense does appear in DDD, the Credit/No-Credit flag for the prescribing physician determines whether it generates IC credit.
steward_steps: Pull the 867 transaction record for the shipment alert: confirm the ship-to ID, the distributor, the product NDC, the quantity shipped, and the ship date.
Cross-reference the ship-to ID against the 867 ship-to territory mapping table: confirm it is mapped to this rep's territory and that the mapping is current.

Identify the specialty pharmacy or account associated with the ship-to ID: pull a list of HCPs affiliated with this account from Affiliations OneKey. These are the prescribers under whom the eventual DDD dispense will be attributed.

For each affiliated HCP: confirm specialty code in IQVIA OneKey and Credit flag in the sales crediting flag file. Identify which are creditable and which are not - this tells the rep exactly which prescribers' dispenses will count toward IC when they appear.

Pull DDD data for the same account and affiliated prescribers for the most recent available period: confirm whether any dispense has yet been recorded post-shipment date, and if not, note the expected DDD reporting lag (typically 4-6 weeks from dispense date).

Confirm there is no feed mapping gap: verify the specialty pharmacy's distributor ID is registered in the 867 mapping table and that DDD data for this pharmacy network is actively flowing into the territory's dashboard.

If a mapping gap is identified, submit a DCR. If the data is simply within the normal lag window, communicate the expected timeline to the rep and advise them on which prescribers to watch for the dispense attribution.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: The shipment alert from 02/18 confirms that 30 units of Sunlenca were shipped from AmerisourceBergen to Pacific Infusion Center (Ship-To ID: SP-7741, 1240 Mission Street, San Francisco) on that date. This is a 867 sell-in event - it confirms inventory arrived at the infusion center. It does not mean a dispense has occurred yet. The DDD data on your dashboard captures dispensed prescriptions - a separate event that happens when a patient receives their injection at the infusion center and the transaction is reported to IQVIA. There is typically a 4-6 week lag between the infusion date and when it appears in DDD. With a ship date of 02/18, the earliest you would expect to see the corresponding dispense in your dashboard is around 03/18 to 03/28. We pulled the affiliated HCP list for Pacific Infusion Center from Affiliations OneKey. The two active prescribers are Dr. Anita Sharma (NPI 1847203948, Infectious Disease, code 44, Credit - fully counts toward your IC) and Dr. Brian Kessler (NPI 1739482015, Internal Medicine, code 11, Credit - fully counts toward your IC). Both are creditable specialties. When the dispense appears in DDD, it will be attributed to one of these two physicians and will generate IC credit for Territory SFO-09. Ship-To SP-7741 is correctly mapped to Territory SFO-09 in the 867 configuration - there is no feed mapping gap. We recommend checking your dashboard again on 03/18. If the dispense has not appeared by 03/28, re-open this inquiry and we will escalate to IQVIA directly to investigate whether the infusion center's reporting is delayed.

inquiries.title: Account Classification Question - Rep Cannot Find Volume and Doesn't Know if Account is Retail or Non-Retail
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I cover Territory NYC-22 and I've been trying to track down volume for Harlem HIV Care Center on 125th Street. I can't find it anywhere at the prescriber level in my dashboard. My district manager says there should be Biktarvy volume there but I see nothing. Is this account retail or non-retail? And am I supposed to be getting credit for it?"
inquiries.what_happened: Account classification - retail vs. non-retail, reporting vs. non-reporting - is one of the most frequently asked but poorly understood topics in Gilead's HIV field data. Reps are accustomed to seeing prescriber-level dispensed data in DDD for their retail pharmacy accounts. When they cannot find prescriber-level volume for a specific account, it is often because the account is classified as non-retail, meaning its volume is reported at the outlet level (the account or location) rather than attributed to an individual prescriber NPI. Non-retail accounts include specialty clinics, hospital outpatient departments, FQHCs, and institutional accounts where product is purchased and administered or dispensed by the institution rather than by an individual pharmacy on behalf of a specific prescriber. In these settings, IQVIA captures volume at the account or outlet level - you will see it in Exponent (IQVIA's non-retail data) rather than in DDD (retail prescriber-level data). A 'reporting account' is one whose data appears in IQVIA's data feeds. A 'non-reporting account' is one whose data is either suppressed (due to data-sharing restrictions with IQVIA) or projected (IQVIA estimates volume based on models rather than actual transaction data). Specialty crediting context: even in non-retail settings, credit eligibility depends on the prescribing physician's specialty. If the account has multiple prescribers, the steward must identify which are creditable specialties - ID, Internal Medicine, HIV Medicine, Family Medicine - as only their attributed volume generates IC credit.
steward_steps: Identify the account in question: pull the HCO record from IQVIA OneKey using the account name or address. Capture the HCO ID, affiliated prescribers, and account type classification.

Check the account's classification in IQVIA Exponent and DDD: determine whether the account has a retail presence (volume in DDD at prescriber level), a non-retail presence (volume in Exponent at outlet level), or both.

If the account appears in Exponent: pull outlet-level volume for Gilead HIV products. Confirm the outlet ship-to ID and cross-reference against the 867 ship-to territory mapping table to verify it is mapped to the rep's territory.

If the account is non-reporting: confirm whether IQVIA projects volume for this account or whether data is fully suppressed. Communicate to the rep that actual transaction data is unavailable and whether projected data exists.

Pull the affiliated HCP list from Affiliations OneKey for this account: identify which prescribers are associated with the outlet. For each, confirm specialty code in IQVIA OneKey and Credit flag in the sales crediting flag file.
Check the 867 data for the ship-to ID associated with this account: confirm whether shipments are flowing to this location and whether they are being credited to the rep's territory.
If the crediting configuration does not correctly attribute non-retail outlet volume to the rep despite the account being in their territory, submit a DCR to Sales Ops for crediting rule review.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We looked up Harlem HIV Care Center in IQVIA OneKey (HCO ID: HCO-4421, 2082 Adam Clayton Powell Jr. Blvd, New York, NY 10027). This account is classified as non-retail - it is a specialty HIV clinic that purchases and administers product directly rather than dispensing through a retail or specialty pharmacy. This is why you cannot find prescriber-level volume for this account in your standard DDD dashboard view. Non-retail volume for this account is captured at the outlet level in IQVIA Exponent, not in DDD. We pulled the Exponent data for HCO-4421: the account shows 41 units of Biktarvy dispensed in the last 90 days, attributed to the outlet as a whole rather than to individual prescribers. The outlet Ship-To ID for this account is SP-3318. We checked the 867 ship-to territory mapping table - SP-3318 is correctly mapped to Territory NYC-22. Shipments to this location are flowing to your territory and you should be receiving IC credit for the outlet-level volume. The affiliated prescribers at this account are Dr. James Osei (NPI 1748203957, Infectious Disease, code 44, Credit) and Dr. Fatima Al-Hassan (NPI 1639482011, HIV Medicine, code 45, Credit). Both are fully creditable specialties - the 41 units of Biktarvy dispensed through this outlet are eligible for IC credit in Territory NYC-22. If the volume is not appearing in your IC attainment, the likely cause is that the dashboard view you are using only shows DDD retail data. Ask your Sales Ops contact to confirm whether your IC dashboard is configured to include Exponent non-retail volume for NYC-22, and submit a DCR if the configuration needs to be updated.

inquiries.title: Data Gap from Market or External Event - Rep Sees Unexplained Drop in Subnational Data
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I'm in Territory HOU-05 covering the Houston metro. My Biktarvy numbers for February were down almost 40% from January and I have no explanation. My prescribers are the same, I've been in the field every week, and nothing changed in my territory. Dr. Nguyen alone should have had at least 15 new patient starts in February. Something is wrong with the data."
inquiries.what_happened: Periodically, subnational and national sales data feeds are affected by external events that are entirely unrelated to a rep's prescribing activity or territory configuration. These events include: regional or national data provider disruptions (e.g., a cyberattack on a specialty pharmacy chain or data aggregator), natural disasters (hurricanes, wildfires, major winter storms) that disrupt pharmacy operations in specific geographies, and systemic data feed outages at the distributor or IQVIA level that temporarily suppress or delay transaction reporting. When these events occur, the data gap can look identical to a territory problem, a crediting error, or a prescriber behavior change - but the root cause is an external disruption to the data supply chain. A well-known example within Gilead: a cyberattack on a major specialty pharmacy network in 2024 caused a significant and sustained gap in subnational prescription data across multiple therapeutic areas. Another example: a hurricane affecting the Gulf Coast caused a multi-week data outage for specialty pharmacies in the affected region, suppressing both 867 and DDD data for the impacted geography. Specialty crediting context: a data gap from an external event does not change whether an HCP is creditable or non-creditable. However, it does mean that even creditable prescribers' scripts are missing from the data - not because they did not prescribe, but because the transactions were not reported.
steward_steps: Pull the rep's DDD and 867 transaction history for the territory week-by-week for the past 60 days: identify exactly when the volume drop began and whether it is a complete blackout or a partial suppression.

Check whether the data gap is isolated to this territory or is visible across multiple territories in the same region or sharing the same specialty pharmacy network. A widespread pattern strongly indicates an external event rather than a territory-level issue.

Review any data advisory or known issue communications from IQVIA, the distributor, or Gilead's data operations team for the affected time period. Known events such as cyberattacks, natural disasters, or pharmacy network outages are typically communicated via internal data advisory notices.

Cross-reference the affected geography or pharmacy network against any publicly known events (e.g., HRSA advisories, pharmacy chain announcements, federal emergency declarations for natural disasters) that could explain the data disruption.
Confirm the rep's territory alignment, crediting flags, and feed mapping are all correct and unchanged - rule out a configuration change as a contributing factor.
If an external data event is confirmed, document the affected period, the estimated missing volume by product and prescriber, and the data recovery timeline from IQVIA.

Escalate to Sales Ops and the IC team with the data gap documentation: determine whether a retroactive data correction will be issued by IQVIA, and whether an IC quota adjustment is warranted for the affected period.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We pulled the week-by-week DDD and 867 transaction history for Territory HOU-05 over the past 60 days. Your territory showed normal volume through the week of 01/27 and then dropped sharply beginning the week of 02/03 - a near-complete blackout across all prescribers, not just Dr. Nguyen. This pattern rules out a territory configuration change or a single prescriber issue. It is consistent with an external data supply chain disruption. After reviewing internal data advisories, we confirmed the root cause: a cyberattack on Shields Health Solutions - a specialty pharmacy network that handles a significant portion of HIV injectable and oral prescriptions in the Gulf Coast region - began on 02/01/2024 and disrupted their reporting feed to IQVIA for approximately 6 weeks. Territories across Texas, Louisiana, and Oklahoma were affected. HOU-05 routes approximately 60% of its Biktarvy volume through Shields-affiliated pharmacies, which explains the near-40% overall territory drop you observed. This is a data supply chain issue, not a field performance issue. Dr. Nguyen (NPI 1748203946, Infectious Disease, code 44, Credit), along with all other creditable prescribers in HOU-05, has not changed their prescribing behavior. The transactions occurred - they simply were not reported into the IQVIA feed during the disruption window. IQVIA has committed to a retroactive data correction for the February 03 - March 14 period. Based on the advisory, corrected data is expected to load by 03/28. We have documented the affected period and estimated missing volume for HOU-05 - approximately 62 units of Biktarvy - and submitted this to the IC team for quota adjustment consideration. You will be notified when the corrected data loads and whether an IC adjustment will be applied.

inquiries.title: HCP Flagged Do Not Contact - Rep Disputes the Flag
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I cover Territory BOS-03 and I've been seeing Dr. Karen Mitchell at Boston ID Associates every other week for eight months - she's one of my most engaged HIV prescribers. Today I went to log my call and the CRM is blocking me with a Do Not Contact flag on her profile. She has never asked me to stop visiting. I have a call plan compliance deadline at the end of this week and I cannot afford to miss her."
inquiries.what_happened: Dr. Karen Mitchell (NPI 1586047293) has a Do Not Contact flag active in the CRM, triggered two weeks ago when she clicked 'unsubscribe' on a Gilead promotional email. The marketing automation system interpreted the unsubscribe as a blanket DNC and propagated it to the CRM, blocking all interaction types including in-person field call logging. Per Gilead's DNC policy, an email unsubscribe restricts email and digital promotional contact only - it does not constitute a request to cease in-person field visits unless the HCP explicitly requests that in writing. Specialty crediting context: Dr. Mitchell is an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44) - a fully creditable specialty for all Gilead HIV treatment products. Her Credit flag is correctly set. Every missed call against her not only affects compliance metrics but also represents lost engagement with one of the rep's highest-value creditable prescribers. The compliance period closes in 4 days.
steward_steps: Pull the DNC flag detail for NPI 1586047293: identify the flag source, flag type, and the exact date it was set.
Review the marketing opt-out log: confirm the flag originated from the Gilead email campaign unsubscribe on the date in question.
Cross-reference with the compliance and legal DNC policy: confirm an email unsubscribe is a scoped opt-out (email/digital only) and does not restrict in-person detailing.
If confirmed as email-only scope, submit an urgent DCR to scope the DNC flag to 'Email Only,' restoring field call logging. Do NOT remove the flag entirely.
Confirm Dr. Mitchell's Credit flag remains active in the sales crediting flag file - the DNC flag should have no effect on credit eligibility, only on contact permissions.
Notify the rep immediately upon DCR submission so they can plan their field schedule ahead of the compliance deadline.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We traced the DNC flag to a Gilead email campaign unsubscribe by Dr. Mitchell on 02/28. Per Gilead's DNC policy (Section 4.2), an email unsubscribe restricts email and digital promotional contact only - it does not constitute a request to cease in-person field visits. Urgent DCR #DCR-2024-1318 has been submitted to scope the DNC flag to 'Email Only,' restoring your ability to log field calls. Given your compliance deadline, this is being processed on an expedited basis - the scoped flag will be active within 4 hours. We confirmed Dr. Mitchell's Credit flag in the sales crediting flag file is unaffected. She remains a fully creditable Infectious Disease specialist (code 44). The DNC flag change affected contact permissions only - not her credit eligibility. You will receive a system notification when access is restored. Email and digital promotional outreach to Dr. Mitchell remains restricted and must continue to be honored.

inquiries.title: HCP Missing from Rep's Territory After ZIP Code-to-Territory Realignment
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I've been calling on Dr. Patricia Lawson at Northside Infectious Disease for over a year - she is one of my strongest HIV prescribers. This morning I logged into my CRM and she's completely gone from my territory list. I can't pull her up under my accounts at all. Did something change in the realignment over the weekend?"
inquiries.what_happened: A territory realignment was processed over the weekend and the alignment file was refreshed in the CRM. Dr. Lawson's practice address sits in ZIP code 30342, which was reassigned to an adjacent territory during the zip code-to-territory restructure. Under the prior alignment, ZIP 30342 mapped to Territory ATL-07 (this rep). Post-realignment, ZIP 30342 moved to Territory ATL-12, belonging to a different rep. The CRM alignment sync ran Sunday night and removed Dr. Lawson from the original rep's view. Specialty crediting context: Dr. Lawson is an Infectious Disease specialist (specialty code 44). Infectious Disease is a fully creditable specialty under Gilead's HIV treatment portfolio (Biktarvy, Sunlenca, Descovy treatment). The rep was receiving sales credit for every script she wrote. If she is permanently reassigned to ATL-12, the original rep loses all future credit from one of their highest-volume creditable prescribers.
steward_steps: Pull the current alignment file and filter for Dr. Lawson's NPI (1467823905). Confirm which territory she is now mapped to and the effective date of the zip code-to-territory change.
Check the DCR log: was a zip code-to-territory boundary change DCR submitted prior to the realignment? Review the effective date and whether any keep-with-rep exception was flagged.
Open IQVIA OneKey and verify Dr. Lawson's primary practice address. Confirm ZIP code 30342 and cross-check the zip code-to-territory mapping in the current alignment file.

Review the sales crediting flag file: confirm Dr. Lawson's specialty code (44 - Infectious Disease) is on the creditable specialty list for Biktarvy and Sunlenca, and that her Credit flag was active for this rep's territory before the realignment.
Pull IQVIA DDD/XPO: confirm Dr. Lawson's Gilead HIV prescribing volume over the last 6 months to quantify the business and credit impact of the territory move.
If the rep has a legitimate business case (primary relationship, fully creditable prescriber, volume), escalate to Sales Ops for an alignment exception review with supporting evidence from steps 1-4.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: Dr. Lawson was moved to Territory ATL-12 as part of the March realignment effective 03/01. Her practice ZIP code 30342 was reassigned to Territory ATL-12 in the updated alignment file. Her address in IQVIA OneKey is unchanged - this was a zip code-to-territory boundary change only, not a data error on her profile. Important: as an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44), Dr. Lawson is a fully creditable prescriber under Gilead's HIV treatment portfolio. The rep was receiving sales credit for all of her Biktarvy and Sunlenca scripts. This is not an HCP where partial credit applies - it is full credit or none, depending on territory ownership. We have reviewed her 6-month DDD prescribing history and flagged this as a high-impact creditable HCP transfer for Sales Ops review. A territory exception request has been submitted to your Regional Director - decision within 3 business days. Your call history against Dr. Lawson is preserved in the CRM regardless of the territory assignment outcome.

inquiries.title: Territory Expanded but New ZIP Code HCPs Not Appearing in CRM
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I got the email saying my territory expanded to include the downtown corridor ZIP codes, but my CRM still shows the same 47 HCPs I had before. The new doctors in ZIP 10013 that I'm supposed to be calling on aren't showing up anywhere on my dashboard. How am I supposed to hit my call plan if I can't even see my targets?"
inquiries.what_happened: A territory expansion was communicated to the rep with an effective date of the first of the month. However, the CRM alignment refresh did not process the new ZIP code additions correctly. The DCR submitted to add ZIP code 10013 to the rep's territory referenced an outdated territory configuration code. The zip code-to-territory mapping in the alignment file did not update, and no new HCPs from ZIP 10013 appeared in the rep's CRM target list. There are 23 HCPs in ZIP 10013 with active Gilead HIV prescribing history per DDD data. However, not all of them will generate sales credit for the rep. Of the 23, the specialty crediting rules for Gilead HIV treatment products (Biktarvy, Sunlenca) flag 14 as creditable specialties: Infectious Disease, HIV Medicine, Internal Medicine, and Family Medicine. The remaining 9 include Psychiatrists, Dermatologists, and a Neurologist - all non-creditable specialties for HIV treatment. The rep's call plan and IC impact should be assessed against the 14 creditable HCPs, not the full 23.
steward_steps: Retrieve the original DCR submitted for this territory expansion. Verify the territory configuration code referenced matches the current zip code-to-territory structure in the alignment file.
Cross-reference the alignment file to confirm which territory ID ZIP code 10013 is currently mapped to and whether it was correctly linked to this rep.
Pull IQVIA OneKey for all HCPs in ZIP 10013 and extract specialty codes for each.

Apply the Gilead HIV treatment creditable specialty list to the full HCP set: flag each HCP as Credit or No-Credit. Creditable for HIV treatment: Infectious Disease (44), HIV Medicine (45), Internal Medicine (11), Family Medicine (01), General Practice (02). Non-creditable: Psychiatry (26), Dermatology (07), Neurology (13), Oncology (22), and any specialty not on the approved list.
Cross-reference the creditable HCPs with DDD to confirm active Gilead HIV Rx volume - these are the priority additions to the rep's call plan.
Submit a corrected DCR with the accurate territory configuration code and original effective date, flagged as urgent with retroactive start date.
Provide the rep and District Manager a clear breakdown: total HCPs added, creditable count, non-creditable count, and estimated IC impact based on creditable prescribers only.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: Root cause confirmed: the DCR for your territory expansion referenced an outdated territory configuration code. The zip code-to-territory mapping never updated, which is why ZIP 10013 HCPs did not appear in your CRM. Corrected DCR #DCR-2024-1187 has been submitted, effective retroactive to your expansion date of 03/01. All 23 HCPs in ZIP 10013 will be added to your CRM within 24-48 hours. However, it is important to understand the specialty crediting breakdown: of the 23 HCPs, 14 are in creditable specialties for Gilead HIV treatment products (Infectious Disease, HIV Medicine, Internal Medicine, Family Medicine) and will generate sales credit for you when they prescribe. The remaining 9 - including 4 Psychiatrists, 3 Dermatologists, and 1 Neurologist - are non-creditable specialties for the HIV treatment portfolio. Their prescribing will show on your DDD dashboard but will not count toward your IC. Your call plan expectations and IC baseline should be sized against the 14 creditable HCPs. This breakdown has been shared with your District Manager.

inquiries.title: Co-Promotion Calls Logged But Credit Not Appearing on Dashboard
inquiries.field_rep_says: "My HIV specialist partner and I have been co-detailing Dr. Mark Henderson together every week for the past month. We're both logging the calls in the CRM and I can see them in the activity feed. But when I look at my dashboard, none of those calls are pulling into my co-promotion credit tally. It's like the system doesn't recognize that I'm part of this arrangement."
inquiries.what_happened: The rep and their co-promotion partner are correctly logging co-detail calls in the CRM. The calls appear in the activity log but are not generating credit on the primary care rep's IC dashboard. The sales crediting flag file does not have the co-promote flag activated for this rep's territory + Dr. Henderson's NPI (1538024679) + Biktarvy combination. The co-promotion arrangement was set up mid-cycle after a new District Manager onboarded, but no formal DCR was ever submitted. Specialty crediting context is critical here: Dr. Henderson is an Internal Medicine physician (specialty code 11). Internal Medicine is on the creditable specialty list for Gilead HIV treatment products - so his prescriptions do generate credit. However, the rep needs to be aware that if they co-detail any non-creditable specialty HCPs alongside their HIV specialist partner (e.g., a Psychiatrist who occasionally prescribes Biktarvy), those co-detail calls will not generate credit regardless of the co-promote flag status. The co-promote flag enables credit-sharing on creditable HCPs only.
steward_steps: Search the DCR log for any co-promotion activation request tied to this rep's territory, Dr. Henderson's NPI (1538024679), or the HIV primary care / specialist pairing.

Open the sales crediting flag file: check the co-promote flag status for Dr. Henderson's NPI under this rep's territory and confirm his specialty code (11 - Internal Medicine) is on the creditable specialty list for Biktarvy.

Pull IQVIA OneKey for Dr. Henderson: confirm specialty code 11 is correctly recorded. Internal Medicine is creditable for HIV treatment - confirm no secondary specialty or reclassification is pending that could affect his credit status.

Review the CRM call log: pull all co-detail calls between this rep and their partner against Dr. Henderson - capture dates, timestamps, and submitted records to establish the retroactive correction period.

Submit a DCR to activate the co-promote flag retroactive to the first co-detail call date, specifying the credit split ratio (confirm with District Manager - typically 50/50 or 60/40 for primary care vs. specialist co-promote).
Notify the IC team of the retroactive flag request with the call log as supporting documentation for the IC true-up.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We confirmed the co-promote flag was not activated in the sales crediting flag file for Dr. Henderson under your territory. No formal DCR was submitted when the arrangement was established. We verified Dr. Henderson's specialty in IQVIA OneKey: Internal Medicine (code 11). Internal Medicine is a fully creditable specialty for Gilead HIV treatment products - his Biktarvy and Descovy treatment scripts generate sales credit. The co-promote flag will correctly enable credit-sharing between you and your partner for his scripts. DCR #DCR-2024-1203 has been submitted to activate the co-promote flag retroactive to 02/03, your first co-detail call date. Your CRM log shows 9 qualifying co-detail calls during the affected period - these have been sent to the IC team for recalculation. One important note going forward: the co-promote credit arrangement applies only to creditable specialty HCPs. If you co-detail an HCP in a non-creditable specialty (e.g., Psychiatry, Dermatology), those calls will not generate sales credit for either rep regardless of co-promote flag status.

inquiries.title: High-Volume Prescriber Reassigned Based on Secondary Practice Address
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I've been covering Dr. Michael Grant for two years. He runs an HIV clinic at St. Luke's Medical Center and writes a lot of Biktarvy. But this month he suddenly disappeared from my target list and shows up under another rep's territory. That doesn't make sense - his clinic is definitely in my geography."
inquiries.what_happened: Dr. Michael Grant (NPI 1720945621) practices at two locations: a primary clinic at St. Luke's Medical Center (ZIP 94115, within the rep's territory) and a secondary location at Bayview Community Clinic (ZIP 94016, in an adjacent territory). During a recent IQVIA OneKey data refresh, St. Luke's reclassified Dr. Grant's clinic as a satellite location and updated his primary practice address to Bayview Community Clinic. The CRM ingested this update and automatically reassigned Dr. Grant's territory ownership based on the new primary ZIP code - moving him from the rep's territory to the adjacent one. In practice, Dr. Grant continues to see the vast majority of his HIV patients at the St. Luke's location. The OneKey primary address update reflects an administrative reclassification, not a change in where he actually prescribes. Specialty crediting context: Dr. Grant is an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44), a fully creditable specialty for the entire Gilead HIV treatment portfolio. Losing territory ownership of a high-volume ID prescriber has a direct and material impact on the rep's IC attainment. The crediting flag itself is not the issue - both territories would correctly credit him. The issue is which territory's rep receives that credit.
steward_steps: Pull the IQVIA OneKey record for NPI 1720945621: review all active practice locations, confirm which is flagged as primary vs. secondary, and check the date the primary address was last updated.
Check the DCR log: was any location update or address change request submitted for this NPI? Confirm whether the OneKey primary address refresh was the trigger for the CRM territory reassignment.
Cross-reference the alignment file: confirm which territory ID ZIP 94115 (St. Luke's) and ZIP 94016 (Bayview) map to respectively.

Pull DDD prescribing data for NPI 1720945621: identify which practice location the prescriptions are associated with and calculate the volume split between ZIP 94115 and ZIP 94016 over the past 6 months.

Review the sales crediting flag file: confirm Dr. Grant's Credit flag is active and verify whether the crediting rule is prescriber NPI-based or practice-address-based - this determines whether the address change triggers a credit reassignment.

If DDD data confirms the majority of prescribing activity originates from the St. Luke's location (ZIP 94115), prepare an alignment exception request using prescribing location as the business rule override.
Submit a DCR to maintain Dr. Grant under the original rep's territory, citing the 'primary prescribing location' business rule and the DDD volume evidence.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We confirmed Dr. Grant practices at two locations. IQVIA OneKey recently reclassified his St. Luke's clinic as a satellite and updated his primary address to Bayview Community Clinic - this triggered the automatic CRM territory reassignment. DDD prescribing data shows that 82% of his Gilead HIV prescribing activity over the past 6 months originates from the St. Luke's clinic in ZIP 94115, which maps to your territory. Only 18% is associated with the Bayview location. As an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44), Dr. Grant is a fully creditable prescriber for Biktarvy, Sunlenca, and Descovy treatment. His Credit flag is correctly active - the question is purely one of territory ownership, not credit eligibility. DCR #DCR-2024-1345 has been submitted requesting an alignment exception to keep Dr. Grant assigned to your territory, citing the primary prescribing location business rule and the 82/18 DDD volume split as supporting evidence. Your full call history with Dr. Grant remains intact in the CRM and no IC credit will be impacted while the exception review is processed. Decision expected within 3 business days.

inquiries.title: Duplicate HCP Records Causing Split Call History and Missing Credit
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I was looking up Dr. James Whitfield this morning to prep for my call and I noticed there are two profiles for him in my CRM. One has all my old call notes going back 18 months, and a newer one that was apparently created recently with his current address. I don't know which one is the real record - and I'm worried my sales credit is split across two profiles."
inquiries.what_happened: Dr. James Whitfield (NPI 1023847562) has two active records in the CRM. The original record (HCP-00412) was created when he was onboarded as a target and carries 18 months of call history and IC-attributed Rx credit. A second record (HCP-01987) was created 6 weeks ago when the rep submitted a DCR to update Dr. Whitfield's address after he relocated his practice. The address DCR triggered a new record creation instead of an update because the name format differed slightly - the system did not match them as the same individual. IQVIA OneKey has a single canonical record for NPI 1023847562, now reflecting the updated address. Specialty crediting impact: Dr. Whitfield is an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44) - a fully creditable specialty for all Gilead HIV treatment products. Both records are carrying partial Rx attribution for a prescriber who should have a single clean credit line. Any scripts attributed to the duplicate record (HCP-01987) may not be rolling up correctly to the rep's IC dashboard, meaning the rep could be missing credit on a fully creditable prescriber.
steward_steps: Confirm both CRM records (HCP-00412 and HCP-01987) share the same NPI (1023847562) by pulling each profile's NPI field.
Open IQVIA OneKey for NPI 1023847562: retrieve the master MDM ID and confirm there is a single canonical record.
Pull the DCR log: confirm the address update request created a new record instead of updating HCP-00412, and document the name mismatch as root cause.

Audit both records: document all call history, Rx credits, IC attributions, and the specialty code (44 - Infectious Disease, creditable) on each profile. Confirm both records carry Credit status in the sales crediting flag file.
Submit a merge DCR designating HCP-00412 as the master record, anchored to the OneKey MDM ID. Ensure the Credit flag and specialty code from the master record are preserved on the surviving profile.
Coordinate with the IC team to ensure all credits on HCP-01987 are consolidated into HCP-00412 before the merge finalizes.
After the merge, verify the updated address from OneKey is correctly reflected on the surviving record.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We confirmed both profiles share NPI 1023847562. IQVIA OneKey has one canonical record, confirming this is an internal CRM duplication caused by a name format mismatch in your address DCR - not a real duplicate practitioner. Dr. Whitfield is an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44), a fully creditable specialty for Gilead HIV treatment products. Both records should carry Credit status - we have confirmed this in the sales crediting flag file for both profile IDs. Merge DCR #DCR-2024-1215 has been submitted to consolidate both records under HCP-00412, preserving your full 18-month call history and all IC credit. The IC team has been notified to ensure no credited scripts are dropped during the consolidation. The merge and address update will be live in your CRM within 3 business days. You will receive a confirmation notification.

inquiries.title: HCP Specialty Miscoded - Rep Receiving No Credit for a Creditable Prescriber
inquiries.field_rep_says: "Dr. Susan Foster is one of the most active HIV prescribers in my territory - I know she's writing Biktarvy scripts every single week. But in my CRM she's classified as Family Medicine and she's sitting at a Tier 3 low-priority level. I can't get her added to my primary call plan because the system doesn't recognize her as a specialist. And when I look at my credit report, I don't think I'm even getting credit for her scripts. She's board-certified in Infectious Disease."
inquiries.what_happened: Dr. Susan Foster (NPI 1659034872) is classified in the CRM as Family Medicine (specialty code 01). While Family Medicine is itself a creditable specialty for Gilead HIV treatment products, the real problem runs deeper. IQVIA OneKey shows Dr. Foster holds two specialty designations: primary specialty Family Medicine and secondary specialty Infectious Disease (code 44). The CRM only ingests the primary specialty from OneKey. However, the sales crediting flag file uses the CRM specialty code to determine the Credit/No-Credit flag - and in this case the crediting operations team applied the flag configuration manually and incorrectly set her as No-Credit, apparently confusing a prior specialty code cleanup that temporarily showed her as an unlisted specialty code (code 99 - Unclassified) before the Family Medicine code was loaded. The result: Dr. Foster has been coded No-Credit in the sales crediting flag file since the code 99 cleanup 4 months ago. She writes Biktarvy scripts weekly, her DDD data clearly shows the volume, but the rep is receiving zero sales credit for any of them. The rep's IC attainment has been materially understated for 4 months. Additionally, her Tier 3 segmentation means she is not on the rep's required call plan, so compliance and engagement metrics are also affected.
steward_steps: Open the sales crediting flag file for Dr. Foster's NPI (1659034872): confirm the current Credit/No-Credit flag status and document the date it was last changed and by whom.

Pull the DCR log: identify the specialty code cleanup DCR that changed her code from 99 to 01 (Family Medicine). Confirm whether the No-Credit flag was applied during or after that correction and what the documented justification was.
Open IQVIA OneKey for NPI 1659034872: document both primary specialty (Family Medicine, code 01) and secondary specialty (Infectious Disease, code 44).

Confirm the creditable specialty list for Biktarvy: both Family Medicine (01) and Infectious Disease (44) are creditable. Dr. Foster should be flagged as Credit under either code - the No-Credit flag was incorrectly applied.
Pull DDD data for Dr. Foster: extract Gilead HIV Rx volume for the past 4 months to quantify the credit the rep should have received but did not.

Submit a DCR to (a) correct her specialty code to Infectious Disease (code 44) in the CRM using OneKey secondary specialty as the source, and (b) immediately flip her sales crediting flag to Credit with retroactive effect from the date the No-Credit flag was incorrectly applied.
Submit a retroactive credit correction request to the IC team covering the full 4-month gap period, with the DDD Rx volume as the basis.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We found the root cause: Dr. Foster's sales crediting flag was incorrectly set to No-Credit 4 months ago during a specialty code cleanup. The team misapplied the flag when her specialty was transitioning between codes. Family Medicine (01) and Infectious Disease (44) are both creditable specialties for Gilead HIV treatment products - she should never have been flagged as No-Credit. DCR #DCR-2024-1228 has been submitted to correct her specialty to Infectious Disease (code 44) and immediately reset her sales crediting flag to Credit, retroactive to the date the error was introduced. DDD data confirms she has written Biktarvy scripts consistently over the 4-month affected period. A retroactive credit correction request has been submitted to the IC team for the full gap period. The volume detail and dates are included in the request. Her targeting tier will also be re-evaluated following the specialty correction - she is expected to move to Tier 1, and her addition to your required call plan will follow. You should see the corrected credits reflected in your dashboard within 48 hours and the IC correction in the next compensation cycle.

inquiries.title: HCP Address in CRM Points to a Closed Office Location
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I drove 40 minutes to call on Dr. Robert Callahan this morning and the office is completely empty - there's a 'Space Available' sign on the door. The CRM shows him at 1450 Wilshire Blvd, Suite 310. His medical assistant told me last week he relocated to a new office in Santa Monica three months ago. Why is the system still showing the old address?"
inquiries.what_happened: Dr. Robert Callahan (NPI 1872053946) relocated his practice from 1450 Wilshire Blvd to 2801 Santa Monica Blvd, Suite 500 approximately three months ago. IQVIA OneKey was updated with the new address 6 weeks ago, but the CRM did not sync the update. A 'do-not-auto-update' flag was active on Dr. Callahan's CRM record - set by a previous rep to protect manually added call notes - which blocked all address synchronization from OneKey. No DCR was submitted to manually push the correction. Specialty crediting note: Dr. Callahan is an Internal Medicine physician (code 11), a creditable specialty for Gilead HIV treatment products. His Credit flag is correctly set. The address issue does not affect his credit status, but it does affect field efficiency and call compliance. Additionally, the steward must confirm the new address ZIP code maps to the same territory in the zip code-to-territory alignment - if it crosses a territory boundary, a realignment and potential credit transfer review will also be required.
steward_steps: Open IQVIA OneKey for NPI 1872053946: confirm the current primary practice address and the date it was updated.
Check the CRM record: identify the 'do-not-auto-update' flag, document when it was set and by whom.
Pull the DCR log to confirm no address update request was previously submitted for this HCP.
Validate the new address (2801 Santa Monica Blvd, Suite 500, ZIP 90405) against the OneKey record and the NPI registry.

Check the zip code-to-territory alignment file: confirm whether ZIP 90405 maps to the same territory as the old location. If it crosses a territory boundary, immediately flag for alignment review - the credit flag for Dr. Callahan's creditable IM specialty may need to be re-evaluated in the context of the new territory assignment.
Remove the 'do-not-auto-update' flag (with rep approval) and submit a DCR to manually update the address to the OneKey-confirmed location.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: Dr. Callahan relocated to 2801 Santa Monica Blvd, Suite 500 (ZIP 90405) three months ago. IQVIA OneKey was updated 6 weeks ago but the CRM was blocked from syncing due to a 'do-not-auto-update' flag on his record. DCR #DCR-2024-1241 has been submitted to update his address. The update will be reflected in your CRM within 24 hours. We confirmed ZIP 90405 maps to your territory in the current zip code-to-territory alignment file - no realignment or credit transfer action is required. Dr. Callahan remains an Internal Medicine specialist (code 11), a creditable specialty, and his Credit flag remains correctly active in the sales crediting flag file. We have recommended to the system team that the do-not-auto-update flag be restricted to notes fields only and not block address synchronization from IQVIA OneKey.

inquiries.title: Retired HCP Still Showing as Active Target on Call Plan
inquiries.field_rep_says: "Dr. Richard Thornton is showing up on my call plan as a Tier 1 target, but I found out from my district admin that he retired back in October. I went to his office and the front desk confirmed he hasn't seen patients in months. I'm wasting priority calls on someone who isn't practicing - and I don't want compliance issues for logging calls against a non-active physician."
inquiries.what_happened: Dr. Richard Thornton (NPI 1304857629) retired from active clinical practice in October. His medical license remains technically active and his NPI has not been deactivated, which is why automatic inactivity logic did not trigger. IQVIA OneKey still shows his status as Active because their threshold requires 6 months of zero prescribing before auto-transitioning to Inactive - his last DDD record shows a Biktarvy refill 5 months ago. No DCR was submitted to flag his retirement. He remains a Tier 1 target in the segmentation model based on historical prescribing volume. Specialty crediting context: Dr. Thornton is an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44), a fully creditable specialty. While he was active, every script he wrote generated sales credit for this rep. The issue now is not that his credit flag is wrong - it is that an inactive prescriber with a Credit flag is occupying a Tier 1 slot on the rep's call plan. This wastes high-frequency call allocation and suppresses engagement with other active creditable ID prescribers in the territory.
steward_steps: Open IQVIA OneKey for NPI 1304857629: check current status, license status, and last recorded activity date.
Pull DDD data: confirm the last Gilead HIV Rx date and verify zero prescribing activity in the past 90 days.
Cross-reference with the NPI registry: verify enrollment status and any voluntary deactivation filings.
Check the DCR log for any prior deactivation or retirement flag request for this NPI.
Submit a request to IQVIA to expedite a status review given field-confirmed retirement, providing field report and DDD inactivity data as evidence.

Submit a DCR to deactivate Dr. Thornton in the CRM, and simultaneously set his sales crediting flag to No-Credit (inactive) so that if any residual scripts appear in DDD they are excluded from the rep's IC.
Notify the District Manager so the freed Tier 1 call plan slot can be reallocated to an active creditable prescriber - ideally another ID specialist in the territory.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We confirmed Dr. Thornton retired from active practice in October. DDD data shows zero Gilead HIV prescribing in the past 4 months, consistent with the field report. DCR #DCR-2024-1256 has been submitted to deactivate him in the CRM and set his sales crediting flag to No-Credit (inactive). This ensures that if any trailing scripts appear in DDD they will not generate credit or distort your IC calculation. We have also submitted a request to IQVIA to expedite his OneKey status change to Inactive. His Tier 1 slot removal has been flagged to your District Manager so the call plan can be rebalanced. We recommend reallocating the freed Tier 1 capacity to another active Infectious Disease specialist in the territory, as ID remains the highest-priority creditable specialty for the Gilead HIV treatment portfolio. No compliance concerns will be noted for your prior calls during the period he appeared active in the system.

inquiries.title: New HIV Prescriber Actively Writing Scripts but Missing from CRM
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I met a new infectious disease physician at the county HIV clinic last week - Dr. Elena Rodriguez. She's actively prescribing Biktarvy but I can't find her anywhere in my CRM to log a call. She's definitely seeing HIV patients and I want to get her into my target list."
inquiries.what_happened: Dr. Elena Rodriguez (NPI 1990348752) recently joined the County HIV Treatment Center as an Infectious Disease physician. Her NPI record is active and verified in the NPI Registry, confirming she is a licensed, practicing clinician. However, Dr. Rodriguez has not yet been onboarded into IQVIA OneKey. Since the CRM relies on OneKey as its master HCP data source, she does not exist in the system - the rep cannot log calls against her, she is absent from all targeting lists, and she will not appear in any segmentation model run until her OneKey record is created and synced. This is a relatively common scenario for newly licensed or recently relocated physicians, particularly at community HIV clinics where physician turnover and new hires are frequent. Without a proactive onboarding DCR, the lag between an HCP beginning to prescribe and their appearance in the CRM can run 60-90 days. Specialty crediting context: Dr. Rodriguez is an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44), the highest-priority creditable specialty for the Gilead HIV treatment portfolio. Every day she is absent from the CRM is a day the rep cannot log compliant calls, cannot attribute her prescribing to IC, and cannot formally include her in their call plan. Expedited onboarding is warranted.
steward_steps: Validate Dr. Rodriguez's NPI (1990348752) in the NPI Registry: confirm active status, specialty designation, and primary practice address.
Search IQVIA OneKey by NPI and full name to confirm no existing record exists - rule out a duplicate or alternate name match.
Verify her employment at the County HIV Treatment Center via Affiliations OneKey or the clinic's publicly available provider directory.
Pull preliminary DDD data for NPI 1990348752: if any Gilead HIV prescribing activity has already been captured, document volume and dates to support the priority onboarding request.
Submit a new HCP onboarding request to IQVIA OneKey with full detail: NPI, name, specialty (code 44 - Infectious Disease), practice address, and HCO affiliation.
Submit a CRM onboarding DCR to create the HCP profile once the OneKey record is confirmed, ensuring specialty code 44 is correctly mapped so the Credit flag is applied on first load.

Flag the new HCP to the segmentation team for immediate targeting review - as an ID specialist at an HIV-focused clinic, she should be evaluated for Tier 1 placement based on anticipated prescribing potential.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We confirmed Dr. Rodriguez's NPI (1990348752) is active in the NPI Registry. Her specialty is Infectious Disease (code 44) and her primary practice address is confirmed at the County HIV Treatment Center. IQVIA OneKey has no existing record for this NPI - she is a genuine gap in the master data, not a duplicate or name mismatch. A new HCP onboarding request has been submitted to IQVIA with her full profile. DCR #DCR-2024-1364 has been logged to prioritize the onboarding given her Infectious Disease specialty and active HIV patient population. Once her OneKey record publishes, the CRM profile will be created automatically with specialty code 44, which will trigger the Credit flag for Biktarvy, Sunlenca, and Descovy treatment from her first record load. Estimated timeline: Dr. Rodriguez's CRM profile should be available within 3-5 business days. We have also notified the segmentation team to include her in the next targeting review for potential Tier 1 placement. In the interim, any DDD prescribing activity captured under her NPI during this period will be retroactively attributed once her profile is live.

inquiries.title: HCP Joined Health System - Prescription Channel Changed and Credit at Risk
inquiries.field_rep_says: "My CRM still shows Dr. Angela Carter as an independent practitioner, but she told me herself that she joined Piedmont Health System back in January. Now I'm hearing her scripts are flowing through a different specialty pharmacy than before, and I'm not sure if I'm still getting credit for her Biktarvy volume. Something feels off with my numbers this month."
inquiries.what_happened: Dr. Angela Carter (NPI 1748203956) joined Piedmont Health System as an employed physician in January. As an independent practitioner, her prescriptions routed through Community Specialty Pharmacy (Ship-To ID: SP-4421). Post-employment, they now route through Piedmont's preferred specialty pharmacy (Ship-To ID: SP-8834), which feeds a different distributor channel in the 867 data. SP-8834 is mapped to a different rep's territory in the 867 ship-to mapping table. Affiliations OneKey updated her HCP-to-HCO link in February, but the CRM has not synced this change. Three of her recent scripts are being credited to the wrong rep. Specialty crediting context: Dr. Carter is an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44), a fully creditable specialty for all Gilead HIV treatment products. Her Credit flag is correctly set. The issue is not her specialty or credit eligibility - it is that the channel change caused her already-creditable scripts to be routed to the wrong territory's credit pool. Resolving the ship-to mapping error or confirming prescriber-NPI-based crediting is in place will restore her credit to the correct rep.
steward_steps: Open Affiliations OneKey for NPI 1748203956: confirm the current HCO affiliation, effective date of the Piedmont link, and affiliated practice address.
Pull 867 data for Dr. Carter's NPI over the past 90 days: identify which Ship-To IDs received her Biktarvy scripts - compare SP-4421 (prior) vs. SP-8834 (current).
Check the 867 ship-to mapping table: confirm which territory ID SP-8834 is currently mapped to.

Review the sales crediting flag file: confirm Dr. Carter's Credit flag is active under this rep's territory, and determine whether the crediting rule is prescriber NPI-based or ship-to-based for this product and territory.
If crediting is prescriber NPI-based: her scripts should credit to this rep regardless of ship-to. Investigate why 3 transactions applied ship-to logic instead and raise a correction request.
If crediting is ship-to-based: the 867 ship-to mapping for SP-8834 needs to be remapped to this rep's territory or a prescriber override rule needs to be applied.
Check the DCR log for any affiliation update or ship-to remapping request submitted since January. Submit a DCR to update her CRM affiliation to Piedmont Health System.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We confirmed Dr. Carter joined Piedmont Health System in January. Affiliations OneKey reflects this change but the CRM had not yet synced - DCR #DCR-2024-1271 has been submitted to update her CRM affiliation record. The 867 data confirms her scripts now route through Ship-To SP-8834. We reviewed the sales crediting flag file: Dr. Carter is an Infectious Disease specialist (code 44) with a Credit flag correctly active under your territory. Her scripts are fully creditable - the problem is purely a routing issue, not a credit eligibility issue. The crediting rule for Biktarvy in your territory is prescriber NPI-based, meaning her scripts should credit to you regardless of ship-to. We found 3 transactions where ship-to logic was incorrectly applied - these have been flagged to the IC team for manual correction. Corrected credits will appear in the next crediting cycle. We have also escalated the ship-to override configuration to Sales Ops to ensure this does not recur when HCPs in your territory change dispensing channels.

inquiries.title: Account Converted to 340B Entity - Volume Incorrectly Counting Toward Rep's Quota
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I've been calling on Riverside Community Clinic for two years. But in my last performance review my manager pointed out that a big chunk of my sales volume is from that account, and now someone is saying those sales might be 340B and shouldn't count toward my quota. My CRM still shows it as a regular private clinic. Is my quota going to be clawed back?"
inquiries.what_happened: Riverside Community Clinic (HCO ID: HCO-7823) converted to a Federally Qualified Health Center and became a registered 340B covered entity effective October 15th. Under Gilead's commercial crediting policy, product acquired under 340B pricing is excluded from rep quota attainment. The HRSA 340B database and IQVIA OneKey were updated in November, but the sales crediting flag file was never updated to apply the 340B exclusion. All commercial sales volume from this account since October 15th has been counted toward the rep's quota. Specialty crediting context adds a layer of complexity here. The clinic has three prescribers: an Infectious Disease physician (code 44, creditable), an Internal Medicine physician (code 11, creditable), and a Psychiatrist (code 26, non-creditable). The Psychiatrist's Biktarvy scripts were already generating no credit for the rep under the specialty Credit/No-Credit flag - correctly. The volume that needs to be excluded from the 340B adjustment is only the volume attributed to the two creditable prescribers (ID and IM physicians), since the Psychiatrist's scripts were already non-creditable and not contributing to quota attainment.
steward_steps: Access the HRSA 340B covered entity database: confirm Riverside Community Clinic's 340B registration date and eligibility type (FQHC).
Open IQVIA OneKey for HCO-7823: confirm current entity classification and when the FQHC status was updated.
Review the sales crediting flag file for HCO-7823: check the 340B exclusion flag status and the specialty-based Credit/No-Credit flags for each prescriber NPI at this account.

Pull 867 data from October 15th onward by prescriber NPI: isolate volume attributable to (a) the ID physician (creditable, 340B-affected), (b) the IM physician (creditable, 340B-affected), and (c) the Psychiatrist (non-creditable, already excluded from IC - no 340B adjustment needed for this prescriber).
Check the DCR log for any HCO type update or 340B flag activation request.
Submit a DCR to activate the 340B exclusion flag effective October 15th for the two creditable prescribers at this account, with HRSA database confirmation as the supporting document.

Provide the IC team with the net volume requiring quota adjustment: total creditable prescriber volume from October 15th, with the Psychiatrist's non-creditable volume clearly excluded from the calculation.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: Riverside Community Clinic became a 340B FQHC effective October 15th - confirmed in the HRSA database. The 340B exclusion flag was not activated in the crediting file at the time of conversion. This was a data stewardship oversight, not an error on your part. DCR #DCR-2024-1289 has been submitted to activate the 340B exclusion flag effective October 15th. Important clarification on the scope of the quota adjustment: the clinic has three prescribers. The Psychiatrist (code 26) is already flagged as No-Credit under the specialty crediting rules - his Biktarvy scripts were never counting toward your IC, so no adjustment is needed for his volume. The quota adjustment applies only to the Infectious Disease physician and Internal Medicine physician, both of whom are creditable specialties with active Credit flags. The 867 data from October 15th to present shows the creditable prescriber volume subject to 340B exclusion is estimated at $118K. This has been provided to the IC team for quota baseline adjustment. The adjusted quota calculation, excluding only the creditable 340B volume, will be processed in the next compensation cycle. Your District Manager has been copied.

inquiries.title: Dashboard Sales Lower Than Expected - Rep Suspects Missing Scripts
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I just wrapped up one of my best months ever. I had 6 confirmed new patient starts from the specialty pharmacy, and three of my top prescribers told me directly they've been writing more Biktarvy than ever. But my dashboard is showing my worst month in the past year. There is no way these numbers are right. Something is wrong with the data."
inquiries.what_happened: The rep's CRM dashboard pulls from two sources with different refresh cadences: 867 sell-in data (weekly refresh, 1-2 week lag) and IQVIA DDD dispensed Rx data (4-6 week lag). The rep's strong prescribing activity occurred in the final two weeks of the reporting period - only the first two weeks are reflected in the current 867 load. Additionally, one of the rep's high-volume prescribers switched specialty pharmacies this month. The new pharmacy's 867 transactions are not yet flowing through the distributor feed mapped to this rep's territory. Specialty crediting context is essential to interpreting the gap correctly. The rep mentioned 'three top prescribers' - the steward must verify the specialty and Credit flag of each before escalating. If any of the three are non-creditable specialties (e.g., a Psychiatrist co-managing an HIV patient's regimen alongside an ID physician), those scripts will appear in the DDD pull and the rep can see them on their dashboard, but they will never be counted in IC attainment. The steward needs to isolate how much of the perceived gap is a timing lag, how much is a feed mapping issue, and how much - if any - represents non-creditable specialty volume that the rep is incorrectly expecting to count.
steward_steps: Identify which data source powers the rep's current dashboard and confirm the most recent load date for the 867 feed and IQVIA DDD.
Pull the raw 867 transaction file for the rep's territory for the full reporting period: compare raw file totals vs. dashboard totals to determine whether this is a load lag or a genuine mapping gap.

For each of the rep's 'top three' prescribers: pull their specialty code from IQVIA OneKey and confirm their Credit/No-Credit flag in the sales crediting flag file. Identify immediately if any are non-creditable specialties - their DDD volume may be visible on the dashboard but will not be counted in IC.
Identify the new specialty pharmacy the prescriber switched to: confirm whether its distributor ID is registered and mapped to the rep's territory in the 867 feed.

Isolate the estimated gap into three buckets: (a) timing lag - scripts written but not yet loaded, (b) feed gap - scripts written and filled but distributor not mapped, (c) non-creditable specialty volume - visible on dashboard but never eligible for IC.
Check the DCR log for any feed mapping requests for the new specialty pharmacy.
Document the correctable gap (buckets a and b) and share with Sales Ops. Educate the rep on bucket c separately.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: Your dashboard is displaying data through the 867 load dated 03/01 - the final two weeks of your reporting period have not yet loaded. This is a timing lag, not missing data. We also identified that one of your prescribers switched specialty pharmacies. Their new pharmacy's distributor feed is not yet mapped to your territory - DCR #DCR-2024-1304 has been submitted to complete that mapping and backfill transactions. We reviewed the specialty and Credit flag status of your three cited prescribers: two are Infectious Disease physicians (code 44, Credit - fully counts toward IC) and one is an Internal Medicine physician (code 11, Credit - fully counts toward IC). All three are creditable. The full gap you are experiencing is a timing and feed mapping issue - none of it is non-creditable specialty volume. Your dashboard will reflect the complete period data within 5-7 business days once the feed mapping correction processes. Estimated correctable volume is approximately 18 units of Biktarvy.

inquiries.title: IC Payout Does Not Match Rep's Self-Calculated Attainment
inquiries.field_rep_says: "I went through my entire dashboard line by line last night and by my math I should be at 118% attainment this quarter. But the IC system is showing me at 94%. That is a $12,000 difference in my payout. I have never had a discrepancy this large and I need this resolved before the payment run tomorrow."
inquiries.what_happened: A detailed audit reveals four compounding factors driving the gap. First, a mid-cycle zip code-to-territory realignment on February 15th removed two HCPs from the rep's territory, but the dashboard displayed their volume through month-end due to a display lag. Second, a retroactive 340B exclusion flag was applied to one account, removing volume the rep had counted. Third, one HCP's scripts were credited to this rep due to a stale alignment and then reversed after the rep's manual snapshot. Fourth, the IC plan uses a new-to-brand metric weighted at 40% which the dashboard does not separately surface - the rep's self-calculation used total Rx volume. Specialty crediting is a fifth, underappreciated factor. The rep's self-calculation pulled all DDD-visible Rx activity in their territory and assumed it was creditable. However, two HCPs in the territory are non-creditable specialties: a Neurologist (code 13) and a Rheumatologist (code 31), both of whom occasionally prescribe Biktarvy for complex patients co-managed with ID colleagues. Their scripts appear clearly in the DDD data and on the rep's dashboard - but both are flagged No-Credit in the sales crediting flag file. The rep counted these scripts in their own calculation, inflating their expected attainment. This specialty-based exclusion accounts for a portion of the $12K variance.
steward_steps: Pull the full IC waterfall: raw 867/DDD data → zip code-to-territory filter → specialty Credit/No-Credit flag applied → 340B exclusions → crediting corrections → NTB weighting → final attainment.
Identify the February 15th realignment in the alignment file: confirm which HCPs were removed, their zip code-to-territory mapping before and after, and the effective date.

Pull the specialty Credit/No-Credit flags for all HCPs in the rep's territory from the sales crediting flag file. Identify any non-creditable specialty HCPs whose DDD volume is visible on the dashboard but excluded from IC. Quantify their volume.
Review the 340B flag change log: identify the account, the flag activation date, and the volume removed from IC.
Pull the crediting correction log: identify the HCP whose scripts were credited and then reversed, and confirm the net volume impact.
Confirm the IC plan's NTB metric weighting and validate the NTB calculation in the IC engine.

Build a full variance bridge documenting each factor's attainment point impact and IC dollar value. If any factor was incorrectly applied (e.g., 340B lookback beyond policy window), escalate for correction.
inquiries.resolution_and_response_to_rep: We completed a full IC waterfall audit and identified five factors driving the variance between your calculation and the IC system output. First, the February 15th zip code-to-territory realignment removed two HCPs mid-period. Their volume continued showing on your dashboard through month-end but was excluded from IC after the realignment effective date: -9.4 attainment points. Second, retroactive 340B exclusion on Riverside Community Clinic removed volume from your IC base: -8.2 attainment points. Third, a crediting reversal on one HCP's scripts was processed after your calculation snapshot: -6.8 attainment points. Fourth, the 40% NTB weighting in your IC plan was not reflected in your self-calculation: overstated your estimate independent of the other factors. Fifth - and this is important to understand going forward - your self-calculation included DDD scripts from a Neurologist and a Rheumatologist in your territory. Both are flagged No-Credit in the sales crediting flag file because Neurology and Rheumatology are non-creditable specialties for the Gilead HIV treatment portfolio.
